research.title: Shared CLI core across monorepo packages — commonalities, layering, and scope
research.question: What are the underlying commonalities any monorepo package CLI needs (pi-context-cli today; future pi-workflows / jit-agent-dispatch CLIs) so we do not duplicate code — a single source for what is best single-source-editable, with each package adding its particulars — from a minimalist best-practices perspective?
research.method: Code-inspection via parallel Explore agents: mapped pi-context-cli (bin.ts/cli.ts) generic-vs-package-specific machinery, and the op/tool registration shapes + execution models of pi-workflows, pi-agent-dispatch, pi-behavior-monitors, pi-jit-agents.
scope: src/ tool registration of pi-workflows / pi-agent-dispatch / pi-behavior-monitors / pi-jit-agents
research.findings_summary:
The duplication risk for monorepo package CLIs is real but narrower than "share OpDefinition."

Corrective finding: OpDefinition is pi-context's INTERNAL wrapper (ops-registry.ts), not a shared contract — registerAll wraps each OpDefinition into pi.registerTool. The contract already single-sourced in-pi is pi-mono's pi.registerTool / AgentToolResult, which all four extensions use identically; there is no in-pi duplication to remove. The risk is entirely in the CLI layer.

What is genuinely common (all generic in pi-context-cli today): argv->params coercion driven purely by the op's typebox parameters schema (--field, @file, scalar/enum/JSON, required-checking); global flags --cwd/--json/--yes/--help; the auth-DECISION logic (authGated + --yes + TTY); help generation; exit codes; and the output layer — OpResult (string|{json}|{read}), renderOpResultText, boundedJsonOutput, and the 50KB cap enforced at the output boundary. That cap was bug-prone this arc (FGAP-013/015: the {json}-channel bypass + the read-.data leak); single-sourcing it stops that class recurring per package. Package-specific is small: the ops registry, a buildContext callback (pi-context's DispatchContext + resolveIdentity + WriterIdentity), the writer-kind vocabulary, and which ops are authGated.

Minimalist shape: one small cli-core package generic over a context type Ctx and a minimal CliOp shape ({name, description, parameters: TSchema, surface, authGated?, run(cwd, params, ctx)}); each package bin ~= runCli({programName, ops, buildContext}). Layering nuance (load-bearing): OpResult/render/cap are used by BOTH the CLI and pi-context's in-pi registerAll (tool text), so they belong in a base both depend on — a tiny op-io package — NOT inside the CLI package (that would invert the extension->CLI dependency). op-io is also where the cap-at-boundary invariant becomes structurally un-bypassable for any surface.

Honest scope limit: a reflection CLI serves only request->response ops. pi-context fits today. pi-workflows fits PARTIALLY (simple ops reflect; workflow-execute/resume are long-running subprocess + checkpoint state + TUI — bespoke, not reflection). pi-agent-dispatch does NOT fit as-is (gating is a pi-runtime pi.on('tool_call') handler). pi-behavior-monitors does NOT fit (tools read runtime state mutated by agent_end handlers). pi-jit-agents is a pure library (no tools, no CLI). So cli-core is the single source for the request->response CLI projection — pi-context now, the request->response subset of pi-workflows later — NOT a uniform four-package CLI. Consistent with the per-package-CLI decision (design-ledger 1c).

Onboarding seam: a package provides an adapter tools->CliOp[]; pi-context's OpDefinition already conforms; pi-workflows wraps its request->response tools' execute->run. The cli-core/op-io split is a PROPOSAL — six open questions (op-io boundary, Ctx-genericity vs a shared identity model, the workflows long-running split, an auth-policy hook, generalizing scripts/parity-check.ts to any cli-core adopter, package count) are recorded in the findings_document; pre-implementation it goes through the canonical pipeline.

dependencies: @earendil-works/pi-coding-agent — pi.registerTool / AgentToolResult is the actual shared in-pi tool contract used identically by all four extensions

stale_conditions: pi-workflows / pi-agent-dispatch / pi-behavior-monitors change their tool registration shape away from the pi-mono pi.registerTool interface.
OpResult / renderOpResultText / boundedJsonOutput / the 50KB boundary cap relocate or change semantics (currently in packages/pi-context/src/ops-registry.ts + read-element.ts).
pi-context-cli's reflection machinery (parseOpArgs / authDecision / the envelope emission) is refactored such that the generic-vs-specific seam shifts.
The per-package-CLI decision (design-ledger 1c) is revisited toward a single meta-CLI.
pi-mono's registerTool / AgentToolResult interface changes.

research.title: Substrate portability — export/import bundles vs an external/global substrate dir
research.question: How can a pi-context substrate be made portable via a `pi-context-cli export / import` command (e.g. encode a project-management model once and import it into other projects so it 'just works'), and/or via a switchable-to substrate that lives at an external `path/to/.substrate-dir` global location — which is feasible, what already exists, and what must be built?
research.method: Code-inspection via three parallel read-only Explore agents over packages/pi-context/src, packages/pi-context-cli/src, packages/pi-workflows/src, and scripts/ at branch context-jit-spec-v2 (post-v0.30.0, HEAD be16688). Agents mapped: (a) the existing model-install/catalog machinery (init/accept-all/install + samples catalog), (b) substrate-dir resolution + switching + external-path feasibility + content-identity, (c) the CLI op-registry reflection surface + the parity gate + the pi-workflows workflow-discovery prior art.
scope: pi-project-workflows monorepo, branch context-jit-spec-v2, post-v0.30.0 (HEAD be16688)
packages/pi-context/src/{context.ts, context-dir.ts, index.ts, block-api.ts, ops-registry.ts, content-hash.ts, object-store.ts, samples-catalog.ts}
research.findings_summary:
The prompt holds TWO non-interchangeable mechanisms. Fork 1 — export/import (file bundle): transport a COPY; materialize an independent instance in the target project. Fork 2 — external/global path/to/.substrate-dir: share ONE live substrate across projects. The user's PM-import example is Fork 1, and specifically a MODEL export (vocabulary), not instance content.

Second load-bearing split: a substrate = MODEL (portable: config.json registries — block_kinds, relation_types, hierarchy, lenses, invariants, layers, status_buckets, display_strings, naming, installed_schemas/blocks, tool_operations; plus schemas/*.schema.json) vs INSTANCE (not portable: each block's items, relations.json, substrate_id, root). ConfigBlock at context.ts:39; REGISTRY_DESCRIPTORS at :942.

Fork 1 — import is ALREADY HALF-BUILT, just hardcoded to the packaged catalog. /context init (initProject, index.ts:250) + /context accept-all (adoptConception, context.ts:873, reads samples/conception.json, never-clobber guard :882) + /context install (installContext, index.ts:334, copies installed_schemas/blocks from samples/ with skip-if-exists unless --update :387) IS 'import from a bundle'. Directionality is strictly one-way today (grep finds no export producer). The seam is two changes: (a) parameterize the source — adoptConception(cwd,{conceptionPath?}) + installContext(cwd,{schemasRoot?,blocksRoot?}), default packaged (unchanged), override to a bundle dir; then /context import <bundle> is thin orchestration inheriting never-clobber + --update. (b) Add net-new exportSubstrate(cwd,outDir): strip root + substrate_id, copy declared schemas, emit starter (or full) blocks, write a samples/-shaped conception.json. CRITICAL constraint: the 50KB output cap (DEFAULT_MAX_BYTES, truncate.ts:12; enforced in renderOpResultText + boundedJsonOutput, ops-registry.ts:127-199) means a whole-substrate bundle (100KB-1MB) CANNOT flow through the {json} channel — export MUST be a file-write op returning {read}/prose metadata (parity-check checkJsonContentCap would hard-fail {json:<content-read>}). import fits cleanly as a request->response write op (template amend-config, ops-registry.ts:1083). CLI exposure is automatic: useOps filters surface:'use' (cli.ts:44), zero CLI edits per op.

Fork 2 — NOT RESOLVABLE TODAY. resolveContextDir is unconditionally path.join(cwd, contextDir) (context-dir.ts:142) with no isAbsolute check and no ~ expansion; an absolute contextDir gets silently NESTED under cwd (path.join('/base','/abs')='/base/abs'), and bootstrap.schema.json puts no pattern on contextDir so it fails silently. Every path builder chains through that resolver (config/relations/schemas/migrations/agents/templates/blocks/object-store/locks/tmp-writes), so all inherit the breakage. listSubstrates only scans cwd by bare dir-name (index.ts:692,:709) so external dirs are invisible to switching. Enabling Fork 2 (no debt): isAbsolute branch in the resolver; a bootstrap-schema absolute-path policy; external-target handling in switchToExisting/switchAndCreate; an external-substrate discovery/registry; AND a substrate-level lock — the real gap: per-block locks (withBlockLock, block-api.ts:45) serialize on the absolute block path (cross-project-safe), but config.json/relations.json/migrations.json are written WITHOUT lock, so concurrent multi-project mutation races.

Identity crux (governs moving instance content): oid = sha256([substrate_id, nonce]) (mintOid, block-api.ts:624) is substrate-SCOPED — re-import yields a different oid; content_hash = sha256(content) (content-hash.ts:65) is GLOBALLY stable — enables cross-substrate dedup + integrity. So Fork 1 MODEL export is identity-clean; content export and Fork 2 sharing both need an oid-reconciliation policy (re-mint under target substrate_id, key on content_hash).

pi-workflows reality-check: 'import workflows' is actually directory-scan DISCOVERY (discoverWorkflows, workflow-discovery.ts — project .workflows/ > user ~/.pi/agent/workflows/ > bundled, dedup-by-name), NOT an import command or bundle transport. It is prior art for Fork 2's discovery angle, not Fork 1.

The forks are separable and need not land together. Fork 1 model export/import is lowest-risk (mostly generalization of existing machinery); Fork 2 touches the resolver everything chains through and needs the locking + identity work before it is safe.

dependencies: Node path.join POSIX semantics — path.join(cwd, absolutePath) nests rather than resets, which is why an absolute contextDir fails silently in resolveContextDir

stale_conditions: resolveContextDir (context-dir.ts) gains an isAbsolute / ~-expansion branch — Fork 2's primary blocker is removed and the 'not resolvable today' verdict no longer holds.
An export producer or /context import command lands — Fork 1's 'strictly one-directional / half-built' framing is superseded.
adoptConception / installContext change signature away from the hardcoded samples/ source assumed by the seam analysis.
The 50KB output cap (truncate.ts DEFAULT_MAX_BYTES) or the boundedJsonOutput / renderOpResultText boundary relocates or changes semantics — the 'export must be file-write' constraint may shift.
scripts/parity-check.ts changes its writer-classification / ctx-forwarding / {json}-content-cap gates — the parity obligations for new export/import ops change.
withBlockLock scope changes or a substrate-level lock is added — the Fork 2 cross-project concurrency gap is closed.
The oid/content_hash identity model (mintOid substrate-scoping; content_hash global stability) changes — the content-portability reconciliation requirement changes.

research.title: CLI↔ops↔scripts parity survey — categorical CLI-side parity under frozen in-pi ops
research.question: Given pi-context's three surfaces over one library (the 48 surface:"use" ops, the CLI that reflects them 1:1, and the orchestrator-script twins), why do CLI defects keep surfacing, and can the CLI be brought to categorical capability-parity with the scripts WITHOUT changing in-pi op behavior — enumerating every script capability and the CLI seam that closes each gap?
research.method: Agent-produced comprehensive read-only survey (general-purpose, opus) over packages/pi-context/src (ops-registry.ts, read-element.ts, context.ts, block-api.ts), packages/pi-context-cli/src (bin.ts, cli.ts), scripts/orchestrator/*.ts, and scripts/parity-check.ts. Enumerated all three surfaces; built a capability matrix; categorized each parity gap under the hard constraint that in-pi op behavior is frozen. Two known gaps (FGAP-019, FGAP-020) reproduced empirically against the live .context substrate. Orchestrator independently re-verified the load-bearing counts (48 use-ops confirmed via CLI --help) and read the verdict/categorization sections directly.
scope: scripts/orchestrator/*.ts — 53 files: 24 op-twin parity targets + 16 composer + 12 runtime-demo + 1 launch-support (reconciled; the survey prose's earlier '52' was an arithmetic slip, enumeration is complete and the 24 op-twin set is correct)
research.findings_summary:
Three surfaces sit over the same block-api/context-sdk library: the in-pi OPS (48 surface:"use" OpDefinitions), the CLI (a 1:1 runtime reflection of the ops, cli.ts:44), and the orchestrator SCRIPTS (hand-ergonomic twins). CLI defects keep surfacing because the ops were authored for the in-pi caller (which reads schemas, retries, infers, tolerates verbose output), the CLI relays them faithfully, and the scripts add ergonomics the ops never had — so every latent op assumption becomes CLI friction on first direct use, invisible to unit tests that always supplied the happy-path input.

The survey enumerates 8 parity gaps where the CLI sits below script capability: GAP-1 FGAP-019 (array_key declared REQUIRED across a CLASS of 7 block-mutation ops though the script derives it); GAP-2 FGAP-020 (read-schema --path pages an addressed object node down to its incidental `required` array name-list); and 6 newly surveyed — GAP-3 no human-render/--format mode, GAP-4 no --show-schema contract preview, GAP-5 no AJV error translation, GAP-6 no append-time dry-run, GAP-7 no input shorthands (--writer kind:id / --where field:op:value / CSV), GAP-8 coarse exit codes (0/1/2 vs the scripts' 1-5). These were filed as FGAP-021..026 (CLI-side) and FGAP-027 (the in-pi op residual).

CATEGORICAL VERDICT: full script-capability parity is reachable with ZERO in-pi op changes. Each gap maps to a CLI-layer seam under the frozen-ops constraint: GAP-1/GAP-7 = CLI-pre-call (derive/normalize input before the unchanged op — e.g. extend cli.ts's required-filter exemption from {writer} to {writer, arrayKey} and inject the derived key via a helper mirroring injectWriter); GAP-2/3/4/6/8 = CLI-only-shared-helper (a helper in-pi dispatch never touches — for GAP-2, re-run the addressed read with structureForRead({whole:true}) which skips collection paging and returns the full subtree while still applying the 50KB cap); GAP-5 = CLI-post-call (AJV error reshape in the cli.ts catch). No gap is forced into REQUIRES-OP-CHANGE for the CLI surface.

THE ONE RESIDUAL (filed as FGAP-027): closing FGAP-020 CLI-side corrects ONLY the CLI surface — the in-pi op keeps returning the paged name-list for addressed object-node reads (structureForRead paging at read-element.ts:266-285). Making BOTH surfaces return the full subtree requires passing whole:true at ops-registry.ts:1166, an op change conflicting with the frozen constraint (user-authorized, tracked as FGAP-027).

REGRESSION FINDING: the existing parity-check.ts dual-surface guard keys the script twin by EXACT op-name (parity-check.ts:748), so 29 of 48 use-ops — including the entire block-mutation family where FGAP-019 lives, plus 5 name-mismatched twins — are structurally invisible to it; it also checks only 3 optional params, never a required-but-derivable one, never output shape. A behavioral gate would add, after the static checks: a script↔op name map, fixtures + an execution harness, a required-but-derivable assertion (FAIL if an op's required param is one the script derives), and an output-shape diff (the read-schema --path case FAILs today: op→name-list, script→subtree). This converts the class from found-one-at-a-time-by-dogfooding into caught-at-commit.

Script inventory reconciled: 53 orchestrator scripts = 24 op-twin parity targets + 16 composer + 12 runtime-demo + 1 launch-support; the survey prose's earlier '52' was an arithmetic slip with no impact on the 24 op-twin parity set.

dependencies: structureForRead/discoverArrayKey/resolveCollection collection-paging semantics (read-element.ts) — load-bearing for GAP-2 and its whole:true CLI-side fix

stale_conditions: The CLI gains a pre-call input-derivation or post-call output-reshaping seam (cli.ts) — the gap list and CLI-side categorization are realized and the 'below script parity' framing is superseded.
Any of the 8 gaps is closed (FGAP-019/020 especially) — re-derive the residual set.

In-pi op behavior is unfrozen / an op is changed (e.g. whole:true at ops-registry.ts:1166, or a dryRun param added to append-block-item) — the frozen-ops constraint the verdict rests on no longer holds.
parity-check.ts gains a behavioral phase or a script↔op name map (parity-check.ts:738/748) — the regression-gate finding is addressed.

The orchestrator-script inventory changes — the 24 op-twin parity-target enumeration must be re-derived.

research.title: pi-bound pi-context CLI — porting launch-constrained-pi.sh into a `pi-context --pi-bound` command
research.question: Can scripts/launch-constrained-pi.sh be ported into a first-class `pi-context --pi-bound` CLI command, and what does that port entail — surface composition, the static-tools + per-target-composite union, meta-package SKILL.md resolution, and the exit-code/auth contracts?
research.method: Spec authored in analysis/2026-06-07-pi-context-pi-bound-cli.md; evaluated twice by independent fresh-context agents that verified each assertion against the codebase (file:line + empirical CLI/`pi -p` runs), then the spec was corrected per the second evaluation and the corrections re-verified.
research.findings_summary:
The spec proposes replacing scripts/launch-constrained-pi.sh with a first-class `pi-context --pi-bound` CLI command that launches an interactive pi session restricted to a composed tool surface. Two independent code-grounded evaluations found the core architecture SOUND: `--pi-bound` is currently an unknown CLI command (so adding it is non-breaking — verified: it returns exit 2 via cli.ts:444-450); `list-tools` is the lone `surface:"process"` op partitioned out of the reflected CLI; and the proposed two-package-root SKILL.md union is byte-identical to the launch script's five-directory glob (69 tools), so the static-tools derivation is preserved.

The second evaluation (after the spec was edited on the first round's feedback) graded 28 assertions: 26 CONFIRMED, 0 REFUTED, 2 PARTIAL, 2 UNVERIFIABLE (out-of-scope-for-a-spec). The two partials were provenance/precision, not behavioral, and have been CORRECTED in the spec: (1) the `--grant` missing-value exit-2 is a NEW contract the pi-bound CLI introduces, NOT a preserved script behavior — launch-constrained-pi.sh:77 is a bare `shift 2` that merely aborts under `set -euo pipefail` (:61), so the spec now labels it as new; (2) the unknown-command exit-2 cite was corrected to the two-armed conditional cli.ts:444-450 (a process-only-op arm at :445-446 and the unknown-command arm at :447-449 sharing `return 2` at :450). Three off-by-one line-range cites were fixed, and an explicit note was added that the port deliberately collapses the script's two-stage tsx+python3 context pipeline (:104-109) into a single in-process loadContext call — a replacement, not a behavior-preserving port.

The first evaluation additionally surfaced four architectural omissions the subsequent edit addressed: where `cwd` originates inside runPiBound; the `pi install -l <meta-package>` meta-path resolution strategy (the genuinely novel port piece, previously least-specified); the script's resume invariant of re-deriving `--tools` every launch; and the exec→spawn+exit-relay swap being mislisted under behavior-to-preserve.

Net: the port is feasible and the spec is now code-accurate. Remaining design judgement (not defects): the meta-path resolution strategy ($REPO-relative script vs node-resolution from a published CLI) is the load-bearing open piece for shipping pi-bound from an installed package rather than the in-repo script.
stale_conditions: scripts/launch-constrained-pi.sh changes its tool-union derivation, grant parsing, meta-install, or context pipeline — the port's behavior-preservation claims must be re-checked.
cli.ts dispatch / exit-code structure (the :444-450 unknown-command + process-only conditional) is refactored.
`pi-context --pi-bound` is actually implemented — this feasibility research is superseded by the implementation.
list-tools stops being the lone surface:"process" op, or the surface partitioning changes.
The generated SKILL.md glob set or the meta-package skill bundling changes, breaking the 69-tool union equivalence.

research.title: Making pi-context a globally installable command, grounded in pi's own bin/publish pattern
research.question: Grounded in the canonical pi runtime as exemplar, how is @davidorex/pi-context-cli shipped so the `pi-context` command (including the spec'd `pi-context --pi-bound` mode) resolves on PATH from any directory, not only via in-repo `node packages/pi-context-cli/dist/bin.js`?
research.method: Read-only code inspection of the installed pi runtime (resolved via `which pi`) and packages/pi-context-cli + packages/pi-project-workflows + packages/pi-context, cross-checked against the pi-bound spec (R-0004); npm publish state verified via `npm view @davidorex/<pkg> version`.
scope: npm registry state of all six @davidorex/* packages
research.findings_summary:
Pi's global-command recipe (the exemplar): `type:module` + `bin:{"pi":"dist/cli.js"}`, a `#!/usr/bin/env node` shebang on the BUILT entry, an explicit `chmod +x dist/cli.js` inside the build script (so npm can symlink the command to an executable on global install), `files:["dist",…]` shipping only built output, and all runtime deps declared and shrinkwrapped so they nest under the package's own node_modules and resolve regardless of cwd.

pi-context-cli already matches pi on type/bin/shebang/files/publishConfig. The gaps: (1) its build never sets the exec bit on dist/bin.js (it is -rw-r--r--), which blocks `npm link` and weakens `npm i -g` — FGAP-047; (2) for --pi-bound it must declare @davidorex/pi-project-workflows so require.resolve resolves the meta-package from an installed CLI, which the published 0.30.0 does not — FGAP-048.

Publish state (verified via npm view): all six @davidorex/* are published at 0.30.0 (pi-context, pi-context-cli, pi-project-workflows, pi-behavior-monitors, pi-jit-agents, pi-agent-dispatch). So the base `pi-context` command is registry-installable today (`npm i -g @davidorex/pi-context-cli` pulls the published dep tree); an earlier draft's claim that none was published conflated 'not installed on this machine' (checking /opt/homebrew/lib/node_modules) with 'unpublished' and is wrong.

Concrete paths: (a) local dogfooding — add the build chmod, then `npm link` from packages/pi-context-cli puts pi-context on PATH with deps resolving through the existing workspace symlinks; (b) registry — already available for the base command; --pi-bound additionally requires declaring the meta-dep and cutting a new (release-gated) version. Recommended publish unit: CLI-as-own-unit (pi-faithful, lowest delta), recorded as DEC-0013; the meta-package has no dist/bin and folding the CLI there would be a larger restructure.

--pi-bound plugs in with no extra wiring once pi-context is global (it is a CLI process mode at the bin's main(), DEC-0014). The one global-install-specific risk is whether require.resolve('@davidorex/pi-project-workflows/package.json') and the downstream `pi install -l <metaRoot>` resolve correctly from a REGISTRY-installed CLI — npm link only simulates the workspace-symlink path, so this needs a published-package smoke test (TASK-029/030 orchestrator criteria, release-gated).

dependencies: @earendil-works/pi-coding-agent — its package.json bin/build/files/shrinkwrap is the global-install exemplar

research.title: pi CLI command-form convention — bare-subcommand vs flag heuristic, applied to pi-context
research.question: What is pi's operative convention for making a capability a bare subcommand (pi update, pi config) versus a flag (--model, --export), and what does that heuristic prescribe for pi-context's surface — pi-context update, the reflected ops, and the currently flag-framed --pi-bound?
research.method: Read-only audit of the installed pi CLI (pi --help + pi <subcommand> --help + dist/cli.js) enumerating every command form and its kind-of-capability; the heuristic was derived from that enumeration and applied to pi-context's cli.ts dispatch and the planned FEAT-005/FEAT-006 surface.
scope: packages/pi-context-cli/src/cli.ts dispatch + the reflected surface:use op set
research.findings_summary:
pi's command surface separates bare verbs from flags by the nature of the capability. Bare verbs: install <source>, remove/uninstall <source>, update [source|self|pi], list, config (config opens a TUI yet is a bare verb, never --config). Flags: modifiers and one-shot mode-selectors of the DEFAULT run — --model, --print/-p, --tools, --export, --list-models, etc. Verbs take positionals for their target (update self|pi); a how-it-runs modifier of a verb is a flag on that verb.

Derived heuristic (first match wins): (1) a distinct self-completing action that is NOT the default run (does its thing and exits) -> bare verb, interactive or not (config is the precedent); (2) the object/target the verb acts on -> positional; (3) a modifier of HOW a verb runs -> flag on that verb (e.g. update --self/--force, the planned update --dry-run); (4) a modifier / mode-selector / one-shot short-circuit of the DEFAULT run -> top-level flag. Litmus: 'its own run-and-exit action, or an option/mode of an existing action?'

Applied to pi-context: the reflected surface:use ops are bare verbs (conformant, rule 1); pi-context update (FEAT-006) is a bare verb mirroring pi update, with --dry-run a verb-flag (conformant, rules 1+3). pi-context --pi-bound (DEC-0014) is MISFRAMED: launching a bounded pi subprocess and exiting is a distinct self-completing action — the exact analogue of pi's bare config verb — so under the heuristic it should be the bare subcommand pi-context pi-bound, not a flag. The recast is pi-faithful (config-as-bare-verb precedent), consistent with update being bare, and easier to type. Routing is unaffected: DEC-0014's load-bearing requirements (route in main() before op resolution; exclude from the reflected surface:use set at cli.ts:44) hold for a bare verb — main() tests first === 'pi-bound' instead of first === '--pi-bound'; the pi-bound module is otherwise unchanged. DEC-0014's decision + consequences are token-agnostic and survive verbatim; only its title/context spelling and the spec md's --pi-bound token need revising. That revision is a separate user decision; this research only establishes the bare-verb form as the heuristic-correct, pi-conformant choice.

research.title: CLI write-path UX — actionable AJV validation errors + --show-schema field hint: root cause, fix seams, and existing-filing map
research.question: What is the root cause of (1) non-actionable pi-context validation errors and (2) the absence of an inline block-field preview on write ops; where do the fixes live; and are they already filed?
research.method: Two read-only Explore code investigations tracing the write/validation path (the ValidationError constructor + the validate funnels) and the schema-derivation surface (read-schema/readSchema/resolveBlockItemSchema + the block_kinds array_key mapping), plus substrate read ops (driven through the global pi-context command) enumerating existing gaps/tasks, cross-checked against the live CLI.
research.findings_summary:
Two write-path frictions in the pi-context CLI, both grounded to root cause and both ALREADY FILED (FGAP-023 -> TASK-016; FGAP-022 -> TASK-017, cli-parity release-group orders 2/5 and 3/5; do not refile).

(1) Non-actionable validation errors. The string 'Validation failed for <block>: <instancePath>: <message>' is built at one shared site — the ValidationError constructor, schema-validator.ts:88 — which maps each AJV ErrorObject to instancePath+message and DISCARDS e.params. AJV runs with allErrors:true (schema-validator.ts:13) and the constructor both receives and stores the full errors[] on this.errors (:92), so the actionable data is present but dropped: params.additionalProperty names the offending field (e.g. 'reproduction'), params.missingProperty the missing required field, params.allowedValues the enum set. Every write op (append/update/upsert/nested block items, write-block, append-relation, amend-config, schema writes) funnels through this one constructor via validate()/validateFromFile()/validateBlockWithMigrationForDir()/validateSchemaAgainstMeta() — so a single change makes all of them actionable. The CLI is a pure err.message passthrough (cli.ts:511-523, bin.ts:8-9) reading neither err.errors nor params. Two fix locations: (a) library-side at schema-validator.ts:88 — fixes every surface incl. the in-pi pi -p tool dispatch, but changes the in-pi op message; (b) CLI-side reading err.errors[].params in the cli.ts catch — keeps in-pi byte-unchanged. TASK-016 chooses (b): lift formatAjvError from scripts/orchestrator/file-block-item.ts:184-205 into the catch + a new render.ts.

(2) No inline block-field preview. The derivation primitive already exists: read-schema (ops-registry.ts:1140-1181) -> readSchema (schema-write.ts:327) addresses any schema node via --path; resolveBlockItemSchema (block-api.ts:2266-2295) returns {arrayKey, itemSchema}, resolving the array property + items $ref. The friction is a 3-step indirection: know schemaName==block; resolve array_key from config.block_kinds (read-config --registry block_kinds, ops-registry.ts:896-942); then read-schema --path properties.<array_key>.items and hand-read required+properties. --show-schema (TASK-017) collapses this into one early-return branch in main() mirroring the parsed.help branch (cli.ts:469-472), before op.run, reusing readConfig+readSchema+resolveBlockItemSchema — confined to pi-context-cli/src/cli.ts.

The two are sibling/complementary (reactive error-time vs proactive pre-write), both from the 2026-06-05 cli-ops-scripts-parity-survey (GAP-5, GAP-4), both honoring 'in-pi ops byte-unchanged'. Open decision: the in-pi pi -p tool surface keeps raw AJV under the CLI-scoped fix; covering it requires the library-side option (a) at schema-validator.ts:88, which TASK-016 deliberately avoids to keep in-pi output stable.

research.title: FGAP-046 schema-merge across pi-context update — git-3-way reframed against the code; base-stamping + shared merge framework + hybrid resolver
research.question: How should pi-context update preserve a user's edits to a catalog-shipped schema across a catalog version change — deterministic 3-way merge, agent-mediated reconciliation in pi, or hybrid — and what does the code say about each route's feasibility and cost?
research.method: Two read-only Explore investigations: a feasibility survey of the update/resyncSchema/checkStatus path, then a reframing of the git-3-way-merge model against the code (FEAT-002/DEC-0004 merge design, base-stamping via the content-addressed object store, the pi-bound + write-schema + auth-gate resolver surface). Substrate read via the global pi-context command.
scope: FEAT-002 / DEC-0004 (substrate merge driver) + FEAT-006 / FGAP-046
research.findings_summary:
Decisive code fact: the 3-way merge BASE does not exist on disk — checkStatus stores the as-installed catalog schema only as a SHA-256 content_hash (irreversible; installed_from.assets = {content_hash, version}, context.ts:79), not the body. resyncSchema (index.ts:464-583) overwrites the installed schema (blind copy same-version :478-481; version-gap migrates block-item DATA, not schema shape :534-564) and never reads the installed body. So preserving edits requires base-stamping as a precondition: store the as-installed schema BODY per install. Preferred shape reuses the existing content-addressed object store (object-store.ts putObject/getObject, dormant today) keyed by the content_hash the asset already records — one idempotent putObject at index.ts:747-753, getObject at merge time, NO type change.

git's 3-way model maps on: keep the base (object store), merge by structure not line, auto-merge disjoint + surface a typed conflict set, separate pluggable resolver. Three routes:
R1 deterministic 3-way schema-merge — net-new = base-stamping + the whole schema-merge engine. CORRECTION to the earlier synthesis: FEAT-002 (the structure-aware substrate git merge driver) is PROPOSED and entirely UNBUILT, and DEC-0004 §6 NAMES schemas in scope but does not author the schema-merge algorithm; a schema merges key/path-level (properties by key, required/enum arrays as sets) — DISTINCT from FEAT-002's id/oid block-item strategy. So 'consume FEAT-002's merger' = co-design one shared framework that does not exist yet; FEAT-002 sharing lowers eventual PROGRAM cost, not R1's immediate first-slice cost. R1 is deterministic/offline but cannot resolve semantic conflicts (overlapping edits to the same node) — it surfaces them.
R2 agent-mediated reconciliation in pi — lowest net-new: reuses pi-bound + read-schema/write-schema (write-schema has dryRun + authGated) + the auth-gate (non-interactive refuses, interactive confirms) AS-IS; net-new is dispatch wiring + prompt. Resolves semantic conflicts by judgement; non-deterministic; cannot run unattended (auth-gate refuses non-interactive). Wants base-stamping for an ancestor.
Hybrid (git-shaped) — deterministic merge for clean/disjoint edits (auto-apply) + a resolver (user conflict report OR a pi-bound agent mergetool) on the typed conflict set. Best coverage; never silent-clobber.

Coupling/ordering: FGAP-046 (under FEAT-006, L4) does NOT depend on FEAT-002 (L1) shipping; both consume a to-be-built structure-aware-merge framework on different axes with different base sources (FEAT-002 = git %O; FGAP-046 = base-stamping; the catalog-update axis is outside FEAT-002's same-repo scope). FEAT-006 can decompose the schema sub-strategy + base-stamping as a standalone slice, designed so FEAT-002 later reuses the framework. Hybrid wiring seam: consult checkStatus before the resyncSchema call (index.ts:666); on locally-modified/both-diverged run merge(base=getObject(content_hash), ours=installed dest, theirs=catalog source), auto-apply the clean result, route the conflict set.

Decision frame (the route is the user's): R1 deterministic+offline+high-build+punts-semantics; R2 semantic+lowest-build+credentialed+non-deterministic+unattended-blocked; Hybrid best coverage at R1-engine+cheap-resolver cost. The single asymmetry: R1/hybrid cannot begin without base-stamping AND a net-new schema-merge engine; R2 needs neither (base optional, improves precision).

research.title: Gaps + issues in the pi-context → jit-agents rendering (agent-spec compilation) pipeline
research.question: What are the gaps and issues in the pi-context → jit-agents rendering pipeline — the agent-spec → prompt compilation that injects substrate context (contextBlocks) into dispatched agents?
research.method: code-inspection of both compilation implementations + the workflow validation/type surfaces (step-shared.ts, pi-jit-agents/compile.ts, template-validation, workflow-sdk, types.d.ts, render-helpers.md)
research.findings_summary:
A code-inspection audit of the pi-context → jit-agents agent-spec rendering/compilation pipeline finds the most fundamental issue is TWO divergent compilation implementations that do not share behavior. The LEGACY path (pi-workflows/step-shared.ts) executes ALL workflow steps (step-agent, step-loop); the MODERN path (pi-jit-agents/compile.ts) is used ONLY by the renderItemById tool. Because the main execution path is legacy, every agent a workflow step dispatches gets none of the modern path's protections.

Legacy-path gaps: (B1) no anti-injection boundaries — block content is injected as raw JSON straight into the prompt (ctx[key] = readBlock(...)), so block content is indistinguishable from instructions (a decision body containing 'ignore all previous instructions' is literal injection); the modern path wraps each block in <context_block role="data"> with XML-entity escaping. (B2) no per-item resolution — object-form {name,item,depth,focus} entries are iterated as strings → garbage variable names. (B3) no budget enforcement — long block fields blow out the prompt window; no x-prompt-budget honored. (B4) no cross-reference recursion — resolve()/render_recursive() globals are never registered. (B5) no renderer registry. (B6) it swallows missing blocks to null (modern throws AgentCompileError).

Modern-path gaps: (C1) per-item injected items ({name:'decisions',item:'DEC-0001'}) are NOT macro-rendered — the template gets raw item JSON, not render_decision(dec,depth); macro output is only reachable via whole-block delegator macros. (C2) no declarative 'render item X at depth N' surface; depth/focus on ContextBlockRef sit inert, never consumed. (C3/C4/C5) the pi-workflows type system + template-validation + workflow-sdk still treat contextBlocks as string[] only — object-form entries crash workflow-sdk.ts:576 (path join), surface as false 'undeclared variable' validation errors, and aren't typed.

Cross-cutting: (D1) the resolve global is single-substrate (buildIdIndex) — no <alias>:<refname> / foreign-substrate classification in templates; (D2) no closure-table edge injection (relations.json / lens groupings can't be injected via contextBlocks); (D3) no dynamic/predicate item scoping (all-or-one-item granularity); (D4) the INJECTED_VARIABLES validation set is incomplete (only output_schema+loop); (D5) depth/focus are dead data — delegator macros hardcode depth=0, focus has no consumer; (D6) the renderer registry is block-kind-granular, not focus-granular; (D7) no workflow-level budget policy — budgetWarnings are collected on CompiledAgent but the executor never inspects them.

Highest severity: the architectural split (most features exist only in the unused modern path) and the legacy path's missing anti-injection wrapping (a prompt-injection surface for every workflow-dispatched agent).

research.title: What pi-context is — the framework's generative model (organizing principles, audited)
research.question: What does the pi-context framework provide as fixed scaffolding versus config-declared vocabulary, and how does .context instantiate that general model — as a usage / how-to / what-is-pi-context reference grounded against current code and substrate.
research.method: code-inspection + live-substrate-query audit: an agent-written explainer verified claim-by-claim against packages/pi-context/src and the active .context substrate via the pi-context CLI (read-config / read-schema / read-block / find-references / context-validate).
research.findings_summary: pi-context is a declarative project operating system over typed JSON blocks: the framework is fixed scaffolding, the vocabulary is config-declared. A substrate declares block_kinds (schema-validated JSON files; content-addressed identity via oid/content_hash; schema_version + migrations.json forward-migration), relation_types (closure-table edges {parent,child,relation_type,ordinal} in relations.json — the ONLY linkage, no FK-as-field, each typed with source_kinds/target_kinds), invariants (cross-block constraints enforced by context-validate), status_buckets (status->lifecycle bucket; an OVERRIDE layer over hardcoded STATUS_VOCABULARY_DEFAULTS — bucket vocabulary complete/in_progress/blocked/todo/unknown, no 'open' bucket), lenses (derived_from_field or hand-curated views), and conventions (block-kind prose rules with enforcement+severity, some checked by requires-edge invariants). All of it is surfaced through one op-registry reflected by the pi-context CLI (pi-context <op>) and the in-pi tool surface. .context instantiates this as a software-project model: 17 block kinds (decisions, framework-gaps, tasks, verification, features, research, conventions, ...), 33 relation_types, and an arc model where a feature binds gaps decomposed into tasks — derivable as feature -> task_addresses_feature -> tasks -> task_addresses_gap -> gaps — with governance via conventions plus FEAT-007's convention-articulation invariants. A custom-vocabulary substrate (.context-jit-spec-v2: axioms/concepts/dispatch-modes) declares a different ontology over the same scaffolding. Two coupling points remain hardcoded (context-current-state, context-status; FGAP-017/018) pending FEAT-004's state-derivation registry; append-block-item lacks --dry-run (FGAP-024, open). What any substrate gets for free, without writing code: typed+validated state, stable content-addressed identity, typed cross-item relationships, lifecycle aggregation by bucket, integrity + governance validation, versioned schema migration, catalog distribution (pi-context update merges catalog vocabulary additively), content-addressed lineage, and a reflecting CLI surfacing every registered op. This entry curates the full per-section explainer in the findings_document; an embedded audit verified it against code + substrate — 22 claims Verified, 3 Incorrect + 2 Stale corrected in-body (the 'open' bucket, the FGAP-017/018 status-bucket misattribution, the universal --dry-run claim, the defaults-scope, and the roadmap.json reference).

research.title: Best-of-breed CLI --help + UX for an agent-invoked reflecting CLI — landscape + reusable elements
research.question: What do best-of-breed CLIs do for --help and overall CLI UX, and which concrete, reusable elements should a globally-installed, agent-invoked reflecting CLI (pi-context <op>, ~48 ops auto-derived from a typed op-registry) adopt to up-level help across ALL commands — primary consumer an LLM agent reading terminal output, secondary a human.
research.method: grounded online research (WebSearch + WebFetch of canonical guidelines, exemplar CLIs, and help-generation frameworks; every external claim cited with URL + retrieval date; two fetch failures disclosed not fabricated) plus pattern synthesis for the reflection-over-op-registry case.
scope: exemplars: gh / kubectl / docker / cargo / git / stripe-heroku-salesforce(oclif)
research.findings_summary: Every loved CLI's help converges on the SAME reusable 3-part architecture, which maps directly onto a reflection-over-op-registry CLI: (1) a render TEMPLATE with fixed slots (synopsis -> summary -> flags -> examples -> related -> footer; clap help_template, cobra SetUsageTemplate, bettercli); (2) a GROUP registry mapping each command to a named, ordered group (cobra AddGroup/GroupID — the gh CORE COMMANDS engine; note clap canNOT group subcommands, so op-grouping is custom render work, not a free framework feature); (3) a per-op EXAMPLES field surfaced verbatim (oclif examples, clap after_help, click epilog). Highest-leverage reusable elements to build ONCE and apply across all ~48 ops + every <op> --help: a grouped top-level listing using the SHORT one-liner (our currently-unused promptSnippet) instead of the multi-sentence description dump (gh/kubectl/docker/cobra; fixes today's flat unscannable help); a fixed per-op template with required-vs-optional TYPED flags and a docopt-notation SYNOPSIS line (an LLM already reads docopt); copy-pasteable common-first EXAMPLES (clig.dev 'lead with examples'; the single highest-value element for an agent caller); a RELATED/see-also hint (the natural surface for the currently-undiscoverable pi-bound); --version (+ -V); help-on-no-args and -h-after-anything -> STDOUT; and machine-readable help rendered through the same --format knob. The agent-primary framing is load-bearing, not secondary: practitioner sources state 'agents read --help before docs' and 'agents learn patterns faster from examples than flag descriptions', and require required-vs-optional shown explicitly ('agents will not guess'), structured/parseable help, stdout-for-data/stderr-for-messages, meaningful exit codes (0/1/2/3/4/5) paired with a parseable error_type + recovery hint, and token-economy output (--raw/--compact). clig.dev + 12-factor supply the baseline rules (-h everywhere idempotently; concise help when args missing; format for scannability with TTY-degradation honoring NO_COLOR/TERM=dumb/--no-color; --json/--plain; help to stdout). ALL SIX points once framed as open design forks are DERIVABLE from the CLI/pi-context facts, NOT user-calls: (a) group labels + ordering derive from the op-name partition — Read/Query (read-*/resolve-*/find-references/filter-*/join-*/walk-*), Block-writes (append/update/upsert/remove-block-item + nested, write-block), Relations (append/remove/replace-relation(s)), Schema&config (read/write-schema*/amend-config/read-config/rename-canonical-id), Substrate-lifecycle (context-*/update), Workflow (complete-task/resolve-conflict/promote-item), Process-mode (pi-bound) — ordered read-before-mutate -> vocabulary -> lifecycle -> process-mode last, implemented as a PURE CLASSIFIER over the op name (a new op auto-slots; never a hardcoded curated list, which is the drift-prone parallel list gap-arc-coherence + the parity gate exist to prevent); (b) color defaults PLAIN (agent-primary caller; honor NO_COLOR/TERM=dumb/--no-color); (c) machine help renders through the EXISTING --format (text|json|table, already shipped) — no parallel --help --json; (d) the one-liner reuses the existing promptSnippet (audit the ~48, fix any that read poorly; no new field); (e) all ~48 examples are hand-authored now (best-of-breed/no-deferral mandate; a synthetic floor is the rejected ship-after tier); (f) pi-bound is its own discoverable group entry with authored help (process-mode, not a reflected op). The circulating ~10-32x CLI-vs-MCP token/reliability multiplier is unverified landscape signal, not load-bearing.

research.title: pi-context update: dry-run/live outcome divergence + per-component transactional seam
research.question: Why does pi-context update preview a catalog-ahead schema as resynced under --dryRun but return blocked on the live run, and what is update's intended transactional contract when one schema blocks while additive config-registry vocabulary is propagated in the same run?
research.method: code-inspection of updateContext + resyncSchema (packages/pi-context/src/index.ts) + empirical reproduction in a throwaway substrate (dry vs live update against a 1.0.0->1.0.1 catalog bump whose existing items fail the tightened schema) + substrate cross-check of what FEAT-006 and DEC-0017 already decide.
scope: packages/pi-context/src/index.ts updateContext catalog-ahead arm (dry 1239-1262 vs live 1273) + resyncSchema 599-641 + registry propagation 1391-1421
reproduction: throwaway substrate, conventions 1.0.0 installed, catalog 1.0.1 adds required `rationale`, identity migration, item lacking the field
substrate: DEC-0017, issue-001, VER-025/026, FEAT-006 tasks TASK-034..039, FGAP-060/066
research.findings_summary: updateContext's catalog-ahead arm splits dry and live into non-equivalent paths. The DRY branch (index.ts:1239-1262) unconditionally pushes the schema onto `resynced` and computes the would-register migration decls read-only, with a code comment that the concrete resynced/migrated/blocked outcome is 'unknowable without running it'. The LIVE branch (index.ts:1273 -> resyncSchema:599-641) forward-migrates the block items (validateBlockWithMigrationForDir:601) and re-validates against the new schema; on an AJV failure it restores the schema bytes, restores/removes migrations.json, and returns blocked with empty registeredMigrations (:622-640) — byte-exact rollback. So a dry `resynced` asserts 'would act', not 'would succeed', and structurally cannot predict `blocked`. Reproduced: conventions installed 1.0.0, catalog 1.0.1 adds a required `rationale` the existing item lacks; dry -> resynced + migrationsRegistered[conventions 1.0.0->1.0.1]; live -> blocked + empty migrationsRegistered, schema/block byte-unchanged, installed version still 1.0.0; AJV `must have required property 'rationale'` at /rules/0. This is FGAP-066 (identified); its proposed_resolution is the in-memory forward-migrate + AJV-validate dry-run. Secondary, untracked: the post-loop config-registry propagation (index.ts:1391-1421) is NOT gated on the schema-loop outcome — it computes registryAdditions and, when !dryRun, calls writeConfig unconditionally; in the live reproduction (conventions blocked) registryAdditions was identical to the dry run (including item_derived_from_item) and writeConfig ran, so a live update reporting `blocked` still mutated config.json. The substrate is silent on whether update is atomic across the schema loop and the registry propagation: DEC-0017 decides per-schema independence across sibling schemas ('never hard-blocks the remaining schemas') but not the registry-vs-blocked coupling; issue-001 scopes resyncSchema's rollback to the single schema's file + migrations.json; VER-025 frames the propagation's try/catch as isolating the loop FROM the merge, never the inverse; FEAT-006's tasks (TASK-034..039) leave dry/live outcome parity, the registry-vs-schema coupling, and rollback scope all unspecified, and TASK-038's description frames the registry slice as a deliberate 'disjoint seam … Independent of the schema-merge slices'. General class: every other --dryRun site in packages/pi-context/src is faithful (validate the prospective result, gate only the write); the catalog-ahead arm is the single unfaithful preview. UX intention (the basis for the filed decision): update does two things with opposite risk profiles — additive vocabulary propagation (safe, wanted, can't corrupt) and schema migration (touches data, the only thing that can block) — so the plausible model is per-component, not whole-run-atomic: do not withhold safe vocabulary because an unrelated migration blocked (the literal extension of DEC-0017), keep the per-schema byte-exact rollback as the safety boundary, make the partial application LEGIBLE (a blocked result must surface what applied + what blocked so it never reads as no-op), and make the dry-run predict the live outcome since its purpose is risk-preview.

research.title: pi tool surface + how pi-bound bounds/excludes it
research.question: What is the full tool surface a pi (sub)agent sees, and by what mechanism does `pi-context pi-bound` narrow/exclude that surface?
research.method: code-inspection of pi-bound.ts + cli.ts + ops-registry.ts + auth-gate.ts + the four extensions' tool registrations + pi-bound.test.ts; cross-referenced against the generated SKILL.md tool tags and CLAUDE.md's `pi -p` usage.
research.findings_summary: pi-bound is a pi-context-cli process mode (packages/pi-context-cli/src/pi-bound.ts), not a substrate op: cli.ts:963-965 dispatches the bare `pi-bound` verb before resolveOp. It bounds the tool surface by default-deny via an explicit `--tools` allowlist handed to a real pi child process (`pi --tools <csv> ...passthrough`, pi-bound.ts:305) — an in-process TS port of scripts/launch-constrained-pi.sh. The allowlist (composePiBoundTools, pi-bound.ts:161-170) is the deduped union of three sets: (1) staticTools — every `<tool name>` parsed from the two skill packages' SKILL.md (the extension surface: pi-context's 57 surface:use ops + pi-workflows 9 + pi-agent-dispatch 6 + pi-behavior-monitors 5); (2) BUILTIN_READONLY_TOOLS = read,ls,grep,find (unconditional, pi-bound.ts:32); (3) the active substrate's config.tool_operations[] composites. `--grant <id>` (repeatable) is the only pi-bound-specific lever and narrows ONLY the composite portion (grants.length>0 ? grants : declaredComposites); static + read-only are unconditional. Everything else (--model/--no-skills/-c/--mode) is pi passthrough (pi-bound.ts:76-77, forwarded at :305). Exclusion is by omission: a tool not in a SKILL.md, not a read-only built-in, and not a declared composite is absent — so pi's mutating built-ins write/edit/bash are excluded by default. A distinct second exclusion layer is the pi-agent-dispatch auth-gate (auth-gate.ts:148-161): AUTH_REQUIRED_TOOLS (the per-package authGated/gatedTools union) is blocked at call time when ctx.hasUI===false, so Bucket-2 tools are effectively excluded in a non-interactive session even when allowlisted. pi-bound does NOT reuse the pi-jit-agents executeAgent grant-clamp (child subset-of parent, jit-runtime.ts:483-491) — that is an independent in-process layer. The pi -p --tools path (CLAUDE.md CLI-Access) bounds via the SAME pi --tools primitive with a caller-authored list, distinct from pi-bound's composed list. The complete pi built-in tool registry and pi's --tools match semantics are pi-runtime-internal (not in this repo); the live set is enumerable via the surface:process list-tools op (getAllTools/getActiveTools). Verify exclusion: call list-tools inside the session, or inspect the composed --tools CSV via the exported composePiBoundTools/deriveSkillToolNames.

research.title: install ceremony has no reflected CLI op + enforcement hooks scoped by op-shape not target-substrate (TASK-033 dogfooding)
research.question: Why can a CLI-driven pi-context bootstrap not complete (no reflected install op), and why do the PreToolUse enforcement hooks fire on writes to non-active/throwaway substrates?
research.method: code-inspection (installContext + the /context install slash handler + ops-registry + gap-register-guard.sh + block-pi-context-glue.sh + parity-check) + empirical reproduction (a /tmp substrate: init+accept-all leaves schemas/ empty; pi -p "/context install" observed not to dispatch; the guard blocking a --cwd /tmp append) + substrate prior-art search.
scope: reproduction: a /tmp throwaway substrate, init+accept-all then unmaterialized; pi -p prompt-mode non-dispatch of /context install
substrate prior-art: FGAP-030 (CLI-parity precedent), FGAP-074, the block-substrate-cli-bypass.sh spec
research.findings_summary: A TASK-033 runtime-demo dogfooding pass surfaced two experience gaps, agent-investigated to root cause / shape / repro / class. GAP A (FGAP-088, RESOLVED by TASK-059): the install ceremony had no reflected CLI op — installContext (index.ts:1211) was surfaced only as the /context install slash-command handler (index.ts:3098-3149) and allowlisted in INTENTIONALLY_UNEXPOSED_WRITERS (ops-registry.ts:2172-2175), while its five lifecycle siblings (init/accept-all/switch/list/archive) are reflected ops. A CLI-driven init+accept-all reached a config-complete-but-unmaterialized substrate (schemas/ empty, no block files) with no CLI op to advance it; a subsequent --autoId append threw 'nextId: schema not found' (block-api.ts:2311). There was no non-interactive path to install: pi -p "/context install" does NOT dispatch it (prompt-mode hands the text to an agent that does not slash-dispatch — observed: haiku-4.5 replied the command 'doesn't seem to be a standard command' and materialized nothing), and install is not a registered op/tool. Install was reachable only via an interactive pi slash command. Class: substrate-lifecycle operations unevenly reflected to the CLI (the CLI-parity family FGAP-030 named); install was the last unreflected member of the init -> accept-all -> install bootstrap triad. GAP B (FGAP-089, OPEN — TASK-060): the PreToolUse enforcement hooks scope on op-shape + block-name, never on the target substrate. gap-register-guard.sh (:20-21) matches write-op name + planning-block name/array-key, and block-pi-context-glue.sh (:22) matches the pi-context CLI invocation shape; neither inspects --cwd or the active-substrate pointer (.pi-context.json contextDir), so both fire on writes to throwaway/non-active --cwd substrates outside the convention they protect (it blocked the TASK-033 demo seed). The hook input carries the --cwd text but the hooks do not parse it. Class: enforcement hooks scoped by op-shape rather than target-substrate, firing outside the active substrate they protect — covers both installed Bash-matcher hooks; the not-installed block-substrate-cli-bypass.sh spec is the resource-scoped counter-example. Both gaps were untracked before this investigation; both filed at class altitude (FGAP-088 / FGAP-089), fixes TASK-059 (landed) / TASK-060 (pending).

research.title: pi-context leverage audit: how fully pi-project-workflows dogfoods the tool for its own PM, and the shipped-but-unused vs blocked-on-tracked-gap leverage gaps
research.question: Which pi-context features and functionalities does pi-project-workflows under-use or not use for managing its own development in .context, and which should it leverage to fully use the tool for its purposes — distinguishing shipped-but-unused (closable by discipline) from blocked-on-a-tracked-capability-gap?
research.method: code-inspection (ops-registry.ts, SKILL.md, monorepo + package README, CHANGELOG) + config/substrate inspection via the pi-context CLI (read-config block_kinds/relation_types/lenses/invariants, read-block-page per kind, context-current-state) + cross-reference against .context open framework-gaps/features about pi-context capability
scope: the open FGAPs/FEATs about pi-context capability
research.findings_summary: pi-project-workflows both develops pi-context AND uses pi-context's .context substrate as its own PM system; this audits how fully the project leverages pi-context's capability surface for that PM purpose. Leverage is substantial but uneven. The write, query, and schema-evolution surfaces are driven hard — 93 framework-gaps, 64 tasks, 50 verifications, 17 migrations, live invariant enforcement; the update / resolve-blocked / resolve-conflict arc IS the dev work. But the organizational and derivational surfaces are under-used: ~7 of 17 block kinds sit empty (context-contracts, phase, requirements, rationale, spec-reviews, layer-plans, work-orders) and only ~13 of 40 relation_types carry any edges. Top leverage gaps: (1) No release/version vocabulary — no first-class binding of a version to its completed dev-items + changelog lines, for a lockstep-release monorepo [FGAP-011, accepted]. (2) context-current-state is non-load-bearing — returns 'no active focus' and derives blocked only from task_depends_on_task, ignoring *_gated_by_item gates; the project shadows it with the hand-maintained CLAUDE.md active-arc tracker, violating substrate-as-single-source [FGAP-061 gate-aware derivation + FEAT-004 config-driven derivation]. (3) context-contracts + gather-execution-context sit empty while the orchestrator hand-composes the exact subagent context bundles that op assembles — a shipped capability unused [discipline]. (4) feature/story containment edges, spec-reviews, phases, and roadmaps are shipped-but-empty — the orphan tasks and the unshipped FEAT-001..004 arc have no structural rollup, which is why release-pinning and focus are not derivable [discipline + the derivation gaps]. (5) Warranted non-invariant closure-table edges are systematically under-filed (only requires-edge-invariant-backed relation_types are enforced) [FGAP-091, forcing-function as residual]. Split: ~6 leverage gaps are closable today by filing-discipline alone (populate context-contracts; file feature/story containment edges; backfill warranted edges; use spec-reviews; file phases/roadmap; file session edges); 4 are blocked on a tracked capability landing (FGAP-011 release vocab, FGAP-061 gate-aware derivation, FEAT-004 config-driven derivation, FGAP-091 forcing-function). Critically: no untracked capability gap surfaced — every 'the tool can't do this' is already filed in .context; the remainder is pure dogfooding-discipline debt. Headline implication: the project's inability to derive its own focus / status / release-plan (the recurring operating-context pain) is NOT a missing tool capability but a combination of under-population of shipped organizational vocabulary and the small set of tracked derivation/vocabulary gaps.

research.title: Catalog↔config relation_type drift: task_gated_by_item consumed by a shipped derivation but missing from the packaged catalog — a class with no parity forcing-function
research.question: Why does a fresh accept-all substrate lack task_gated_by_item; what is the root cause, shape, and reproducible condition; is it an atomic omission or an instance of a broader class; and does the substrate already track it?
research.method: git archaeology on the catalog (samples/conception.json) and live .context/config.json (git log -S per relation_type), pi-context CLI substrate inspection (read-config / read-samples-catalog / context-init→accept-all→install on a throwaway /tmp substrate), source trace of the write-time edge validator, and a prior-art search of framework-gaps/decisions by topic
research.findings_summary: task_gated_by_item was hand-added to the live .context/config.json on 2026-06-07 (commit 669890c, to author the TASK-033→FEAT-006 edge) and never back-ported to the packaged catalog samples/conception.json; the one catalog gating-relation back-port (a582939 / TASK-061) was scoped to FGAP-090's three new relations and did not include this pre-existing sibling, so it fell between the cracks. It is NOT atomic: the catalog relation_types[] (34) is a strict subset of the live config (40), missing six live-only relations — task_gated_by_item, session_touches_item, task_advances_story, feature_advances_story, decision_derived_from_item, decision_escalates_underdetermined — each hand-added to live config (for an edge/lens/invariant) and never back-ported, with no forcing-function ensuring the catalog carries the vocabulary the shipped derivations consume and no record distinguishing intentionally-substrate-local from catalog-worthy. SHAPE: on a fresh install-ceremony substrate task_gated_by_item is not merely inert to gate-aware currentState (FGAP-061) but UNWRITABLE — the registration-first write-time validator validateEdgeAgainstRegistry (context-sdk.ts:1496-1502) short-circuits on the unregistered relation_type and assertEdgeValidForWrite (:1548) throws, so a task gate cannot be expressed at all while feature/story/decision gating ships. PRIOR-ART: the substrate did not track this class — nearest neighbors FGAP-060 (catalog→substrate propagation, opposite direction) and FGAP-010 (a build-time parity forcing-function over the code-surface trio, the structural precedent). DISPOSITION: filed as FGAP-094 (class-level, P2) and decomposed into TASK-066 (back-port the consumed-but-missing relations) + TASK-067 (a build-time catalog⊇consumed-vocabulary parity gate mirroring scripts/parity-check.ts). The catalog-worthiness rule is derivable — a relation_type is catalog-required iff a shipped src derivation consumes it by literal string — so the five non-consumed live-only relations are correctly substrate-local. The decomposition surfaced an adjacent instance of the same class beyond this document's six: phase_depends_on, consumed by shipped roadmap-plan.ts (:329/:505/:528) yet registered in NEITHER the catalog nor live config, folded into TASK-066/067.

research.title: Phase-block dev-process control plane: forensic validation of the proposal — the phase block and the roadmap layer are distinct surfaces, and the zero-code core vs named-code-dependency split
research.question: Is the proposal that the empty `phase` block plus already-shipped pi-context elements form a discipline-closable dev-process control plane accurate against the real code and substrate, and which control-plane mechanisms are zero-code-usable today versus gated on named code?

(1) The `phase` block and the `roadmap` layer are DISTINCT surfaces: the context-roadmap-* ops read `<root>/roadmap.json` and aggregate items through a named LENS (PhaseSpec{id,name,lens,milestone?,exit_criteria?}, roadmap-plan.ts:149-156), never the `phase` block nor task_positioned_in_phase edges; the `roadmap` block is opt-in and not installed in .context. (2) phase_depends_on is registered in NEITHER the catalog nor live .context/config.json (it is consumed by roadmap-plan.ts), so roadmap topo ordering is non-derivable today; the planned TASK-066 back-ports it. (3) currentState ALREADY reads the phase block for the focus-string fallback — an in-progress phase becomes focus when no task is in-flight (context-sdk.ts:864-876) — but it ignores task_positioned_in_phase and does not group tasks under phases; phase-positioning-driven focus is enabled-by, not shipped-by, the planned TASK-020 (a config-driven state-derivation registry rewiring currentState; the shipped conception declares only framework-gaps + tasks). (4) gatherExecutionContext (execution-context.ts:143) is contract-driven and walks the relation_types a context-contracts entry declares; the context-contracts block is empty, so the one-call vertical trace errors today and is gated on a filed contract. (5) A phase-completion invariant is net-new — no phase invariant ships in catalog (9) or live config (10); the cited task-completed-feature-complete is the status-consistency template but a phase check needs the inverse inbound-aggregate shape, closer to TASK-047's net-new advancer-completion class. research.title: WASC operation framework — the canonical run process
research.question: What is the exact step sequence and structure of the WASC operation framework's per-task run process?
research.method: claude-history extraction of the last complete end-to-end run (session 6e98b2bc) and a loop run; verbatim tool-call and agent-dispatch sequencing; cross-checked against the operation-system bundle.
research.findings_summary: The WASC operation framework runs a per-task canonical pipeline: EnterPlanMode, dispatch a read-only Explore agent, the orchestrator independently corroborates the Explore findings against source, write the plan, ExitPlanMode approval, file the task plus edges in .context, branch off main, dispatch a fresh gate-first IMPL agent, the orchestrator re-runs the deterministic gate itself (it does not inherit the IMPL's green), then a separate adversarial AUDIT agent verifies each acceptance criterion and ends with the literal token 'Total findings: N'. Done is two external binary signals: deterministic gate exit 0 AND audit findings 0 (DEC-58); the model may not self-declare done. On findings > 0 each finding re-enters the same pipeline (corroborate, fresh re-IMPL scoped to the finding's whole class, fresh re-audit both-directions) until a pass finds nothing. On done, the machine cascade files a verification item, adds the verification_verifies_item edge, completes the task, closes the gap, runs context-validate, then fast-forward merges to main and updates the spine. Three roles stay separate (Explore read-only, IMPL writes, AUDIT adversarial). The loop's terminator is external, not the model's judgment; that is why it completes. Derived from the last complete run (175 steps, session 6e98b2bc), which audited clean on the first pass.

research.title: WASC out-of-scope STOP — firing rate across the record
research.question: How often does the IMPL/fix brief's 'STOP and report if a fix reaches outside the slice' actually fire, and what percentage of full loop runs never encounter it?
research.method: claude-history enumeration of every full loop run (an IMPL dispatch that reached an adversarial audit) across the 4 WASC sessions; verbatim agent-return search for the out-of-scope STOP; reconciled counts.
research.findings_summary: The out-of-scope STOP fires rarely and is run-terminating. Across the WASC record there are 8 clean firings (plus 2 borderline), all in sessions d7310007 and bd501b6f; the canonical-pipeline session 6e98b2bc had zero. Of 70 full loop runs (an IMPL dispatch that reached an adversarial audit), 0 contained an out-of-scope STOP — 100% never encounter one — because every firing halts during implementation before auditable code exists (all returned no edits, clean tree, no commit), so the attempt never becomes a completed loop run. Across all 187 IMPL plus fix attempts the firing rate is 8/187 = 4.3% (5.3% including borderline), so about 95-96% of all attempts never hit it. The 36 audit-finding fix-re-entry loops contained zero scope STOPs — every re-fix completed in scope. The clearest firing: a translation removal blocked because the off-limits planner/forms.py referenced the translated field, so the agent stopped rather than touch a forbidden file.

research.title: issue-003 re-drive: pre-identity stamping throw is the update refusal's root cause; the blocked pipeline misattributes it as item-validation failure
research.question: Why did update refuse a conventions 1.0.0->1.0.1 forward-migration whose items satisfy the target schema by inspection, with dry-run predicting resynced while the live run refused (issue-003's filed residual: re-drive the reproduction with the shipped blockedDetail diagnostic and fix or retire on the evidence)?
research.method: empirical-test + code-inspection: two-cell scratch-substrate reproduction (conventions schema installed at 1.0.0 against the 1.0.1 catalog, 45 rules valid against 1.0.1 by construction, install baseline recorded) differing in exactly one variable — presence of config.substrate_id — driven through the real built updateContext dry + live per cell; follow-through resolveBlocked run on the blocked cell's post-block state; corroborating read of resyncSchema's catch and resolveBlocked's commit sequence.
scope: workflowsPiExtension main at f7d6f819 (pi-context 0.31.0 working tree)
research.findings_summary: The refusal is infrastructure, not validation. Cell A (no substrate_id): dry-run predicts migrated; the live run returns blocked with reason validation-failed whose synthetic failure (keyword 'error') carries the real message — substrateIdForDir: config.json has no valid substrate_id — thrown by writeBlockForDir's mandatory identity stamping inside resyncSchema's catch. Disposition enacted: issue-003 resolved with this evidence; FGAP-033 related as the mechanism; FGAP-115 filed for the conflation class; the pre-identity design question is resolved by derivation from project precedent (ceremonies heal lagging substrates — config-version seeding, envelope convergence, registry propagation — and ceremonies already mint substrate_id at init/accept-all) toward ceremony-entry identity establishment, to be enacted per DEC-0012's requirement of a dedicated DEC.

research.title: Foreign-status derivation blindness — mechanism, per-surface class inventory, and resolution shapes for cross-substrate consumption
research.question: Does pi-context's derived state consume foreign (cross-substrate) edge endpoints — and if not, where exactly does each derivation surface drop them, what is the blast radius, and what fix shapes does the shipped architecture afford?
research.method: Fresh-agent upstream verification of a downstream two-substrate report: every claim re-derived against current source (v0.32.0 + Unreleased, HEAD aafc8dc); two registered scratchpad substrates driven through built dist via the sanctioned porcelain (appendRelationByRef with alias:refname selectors, currentState, validateContext); per-surface code trace of every edge-consuming join; prior-art sweep of framework-gaps/features/decisions/research; design-record archaeology of the 2026-05-31 identity spec.
research.findings_summary: Every edge-consuming derivation surface resolves endpoints via endpointKey (bare refname, substrate_id discarded) against the ACTIVE substrate's byRefname index only, while the write path and validateContext's edge-integrity accept and certify foreign endpoints as valid — a split-brain with zero diagnostics on any derivation path. Per-surface polarity: gate/dependency satisfaction silently drops foreign blockers (a task gated on an OPEN foreign item derives ready and enters nextActions — empirically reproduced, validate clean even with a covering status-consistency invariant declared); derivedRollupComplete returns false for foreign members (a milestone with every member complete derives planned; reconcile/converge STAMP that false status durably); the status-consistency invariant skips foreign targets; requires-edge satisfaction never resolves the counter-endpoint; joinBlocks and roadmap rollups drop foreign counterparts; walks erase the substrate qualifier. Aggravator: foreign keys are bare refnames, so a same-refname local item (the expected case across catalog-installed substrates) silently substitutes its status for the foreign item's — reproduced: a local closed gB satisfied a gate pointing at foreign-open gB. findReferences is the only non-blind edge-read surface. The claimed single seam is partially true: derivedRollupComplete converges its three consumers, but gates, invariants, joinBlocks, roadmap, and walks carry independent active-index joins — the real seam is the endpointKey+byRefname join convention; resolveRef (registry-aware, per-pass cached, shipped v0.28.0) exists and is wired only into validation. The 2026-05-31 identity spec designed §J3 foreign-aware status-consistency (compare-if-readable, warn-if-unreadable — never implemented) and contains no derivation design. Resolution shapes the architecture affords: resolve-at-derive (registry resolution at every join, recommended), derive-as-blocked-until-resolved, flagged-exclusion floor — a genuine DEC-shaped semantics choice (freshness/cost/offline trade-offs); in every variant silent dropping and refname-collision aliasing must be eliminated and the dangling-vs-foreign conflation split.

research.title: Harness exemplars characterized — WASC's deterministic prompt pipeline and SYNTH's failure-derived deny surface, with the portable pattern set
research.question: What enforcement mechanisms made the WASC and MUSE/SYNTH harnesses predictable and non-bypassable, what does each exemplar's model consist of, and which mechanisms are portable patterns for a process harness in this project?
research.method: Read-only characterization of both exemplar projects (hooks, renderers, gate scripts, ledgers, mandates, state files, templates — load-bearing lines quoted verbatim), per-exemplar analysis of shape / enforcement points / bypass surface / operator experience, and a comparison treating the variant's delta as design intent.
research.findings_summary: Both exemplars share one architecture: an LLM orchestrator whose canonical process lives in committed text/code, whose subagent inputs are built by deterministic renderers with mandates inlined (subagents do not receive the UserPromptSubmit injection), whose commits are gated by the full test suite running INSIDE a PreToolUse hook, whose progress lives in append-only ledgers plus derived synthesis, and whose completion claims are verified by a separate adversarial agent and exit codes, with the human as scope authority. WASC (the first model): the phase is the enforced unit; a judgment-free renderer ('cat + sed. No judgment. No LLM.') compiles phase MDs + inlined mandates into IMPL/AUDIT contracts; AUDIT ends with a literally-parsed 'Total findings: N' line and GO/NO-GO is computed, not judged; only the commit is hard-gated — sequencing is codified but obeyed. SYNTH (the variant): enforcement moved upstream to five tool boundaries — plan entry gated on an advance-only-on-clean spec-audit watermark, dispatch serialized one-unit-per-prompt by renderer contract, ledger writes triple-shielded, and implementation commits BLOCKED-BY-DEFAULT without a user-opened, git-ancestry-anchored, expiring authorization grant; every guard traces to a mined failure ID with historical counts; every hook declares its fail-open (absent infrastructure) / fail-closed (gated condition) split; where prevention is impossible (hooks cannot prove authorship) the design pairs the bypass with forensic detection (a state-currency auditor cross-checking claude-history) and user-only self-expiring overrides; the harness regression-tests its own necessity. The variant's delta = the design intent: procedure-as-refusal over procedure-as-prose, authorization as a first-class mechanical object, rules justified by mined incident data, honest threat modeling with detection where prevention fails, and a self-auditing harness.
