research.title: Harness requirements for this project — the canonical process as a state machine, the enforcement-surface audit, and the twenty-requirement set
research.question: What canonical process must a non-bypassable harness encode for this project, which steps are already mechanically enforced versus honor-system, which observed failure modes must it foreclose at which Claude Code enforcement points, and what requirement set follows?
research.method: Corpus derivation: CLAUDE.md (pipeline 0-7, Completion Sequence 1-13, Experience-Gap Handling, dispatch fit), all 14 enforced conventions read in full via the CLI, the 9 injected mandates and their UserPromptSubmit delivery, the 15-file memory feedback corpus, the 3 live repo hooks + registrations, husky's 5 commit gates, the substrate's 13 invariants and op surface; per-state classification MECHANICAL/PARTIAL/HONOR; failure modes mapped to observable signals and hook points.
research.findings_summary: The canonical process distills to a single-track per-task state machine — SURFACED → INVESTIGATED → FILED(provenance) → GATE-VALIDATED → EXPLORED → PLANNED → BASELINED → IMPLEMENTED(subagent) → GATED → DEMOED → PROBED → findings-loop(class-scoped) → DOCS-SYNCED → MERGED → CLOSED → HELD(RELEASED → PUBLISHED) — with exactly three legitimate exits to the user: provenance grants, honestly-underdetermined scope/value escalations, HELD actions. Current mechanical enforcement concentrates at exactly two chokepoints: the substrate op write path (AJV + write-time invariant gate + complete-task atom + attestation) and the husky commit boundary (5 gates) — plus three PreToolUse guards. Honor-system holes: direct Edit/Write on substrate JSON (a parallel ungated path making the op gates bypassable), the ENTIRE verify layer (runtime demo, adversarial probe, iterate-to-zero, subagent-claim verification), plan-mode/subagent-primary implementation discipline, the release hold (nothing blocks npm run release:*), the turn-boundary ask gate (no Stop hook exists anywhere — the observed chaos driver is invisible to every mechanism), and the wiring itself (hook registrations live in untracked settings.local.json; the project mandates.jsonl slot is empty). Failure modes with observable signals: turn-boundary relitigation (Stop hook), skipped pipeline steps (PreToolUse Edit/Write + phase state), self-adjudicated experience gaps (filing-payload investigation citation), deferred findings (findings ledger at merge/close), unverified subagent relays (PostToolUse Agent + turn-end sequence shape), scope reduction (lexical nudge). Twenty requirements: R1-R10 enforceable today as new hook scripts over proven in-repo patterns (substrate-edit block; --no-verify/destructive-git, pipe-masking, and release-hold chokepoints; same-turn sentinel fix; filing-completeness checks; project mandates file; subagent-relay nudge); R11-R17 need one new state layer — phase-state file, Stop-hook ask/convergence gates, findings ledger, session driver — so hooks check 'is this action legal in this state'; R18-R20 cover the milestone gate, status cascade, and tracking the wiring. Design principles: lazy-cooperative threat model, no parallel ungated paths, preserve the three stops, deterministic predicates block while lexical heuristics nudge, filing-pipeline-first before new hooks.

research.title: Work-order dispatch dogfood — the loop cannot run a real work-order today; five breaks root-caused, one architectural fact seen from three sides
research.question: When a real work-order is filed and driven through run-work-order-loop from pi -p non-interactive dispatch, where does the machinery break, what is the root cause of each break, and is the work-order loop usable as a harness dispatch primitive today?
research.method: User-directed live probe (WO-001: a minimal real work-order whose agent must write a file, gated by a deterministic runtime-demo real-check) driven from pi -p; fresh-context investigation root-causing each break against current source with an instrumented completeFn reproduction against built dist; git-date archaeology; substrate prior-art sweep via the CLI.
research.findings_summary: The loop runs end to end without crashing but cannot execute any real work-order. Highest-value root cause (empirically confirmed): pi-jit-agents executeAgent is a single-turn completion primitive whose context.tools is populated only by a phantom output-schema tool — a dispatched agent granted ['write','bash'] receives ZERO executable tools (instrumented: toolsPassedToLLM UNDEFINED), so the demo agent's 'cannot write files' was accurate and every honest work-order must fail at iteration 0. Breaks 2, 3, 5 are one architectural fact from three sides: the work-orders schema (2026-05-27) is a v1 contract — typed input, context injection, output validation, operation-scoped clamp, bounded retry — whose loop (2026-05-28) implements a demo slice on a primitive that cannot act; the schema even asserts present-tense engine behavior (scope 'Used by the capability composer to clamp') that does not run, and ships in the installable catalog. Breaks 1 and 4 compound at the boundary: the sanctioned agent-authoring tool is interactively gated (no non-interactive path for the 8 pi-only gated tools), and no ceremony seeds the <contextDir>/agents/ tier the loader resolves from — so on a fresh substrate driven from pi -p the loop's precondition is unproducible. Only the deterministic real-check behaved correctly, which is why this surfaced as a clean failure not a false pass. The dispatch-architecture choice (pi-subprocess vs executeAgent tool-loop vs output-only re-scope) is a decision the fix must make first. Also flagged: loop/tool doc comments cite frozen .project-era IDs that collide with unrelated active-.context items.
